document: This section reviews literature on explainable recommendation and user-facing justification. Early research emphasized rule-based explanations that map item attributes to human-readable rationales. Herlocker et al. Recent neural approaches seek to ground explanations in textual evidence extracted from reviews or descriptions. Lei et al. (2016) proposed rationalizing predictors by learning extractive snippets that support predictions, which inspired applications in recommendation. In collaborative filtering, attention layers are often interpreted as soft rationales despite known caveats. Ma et al. (2019) analyzed attention weights and cautioned against overinterpreting them in sequence models. Work on counterfactual reasoning reframes explanations as minimal changes that alter the recommended item. Chen et al. (2018) model user-item aspects with attentive pooling. Wang and Li (2019) leverage graph embeddings for explainable ranking. Zhang et al. (2020) align reviews with factors using reinforcement learning. Xu et al. (2021) study counterfactual explanations. Although these methods target different explanation forms, they share a need for faithful alignment between model behavior and presented justifications. Faithfulness metrics compare explanation components to model sensitivities or adversarial perturbations under constrained interventions. Wu et al. (2022) measured faithfulness via deletion tests and gradient-based overlap with selected tokens. Beyond faithfulness, usefulness captures how explanations support user goals during decision making. Kunkel et al. (2019) operationalized usefulness with task success and perceived control in interactive studies. For recommender systems, textual rationales in reviews offer a natural medium for explanation generation. Ni et al. (2020) combined path reasoning over knowledge graphs with sequence decoders to produce human-readable paths. Our work targets faithfulness and usefulness jointly by constraining the explanation generator with causal tests derived from model gradients. We evaluate on two e-commerce datasets with human and automatic metrics aligned to the above criteria. We release code and annotation guidelines to facilitate reproducibility and future user-centered evaluations.

Despite strong results, most models assume a static graph that does not reflect incident-driven changes. Adaptive adjacency learning attempts to overcome this limitation with data-driven connectivity. Dynamic graphs also raise identifiability concerns when edge weights shift with exogenous factors. External covariates such as weather and events have been integrated through feature fusion. Meta-learning frameworks adjust parameters to new cities without retraining from scratch. Transfer across regions is challenging due to sensor placement and policy differences. Robust training reduces sensitivity to missing data and sensor faults in deployment. OOD generalization remains underexplored when traffic patterns change seasonally or during crises. Evaluation protocols vary widely in horizon, metrics, and normalization, complicating fair comparison. We focus on uncertainty-aware forecasting that separates data noise from model uncertainty. Our method augments graph diffusion with heteroscedastic outputs calibrated via proper scoring rules. The approach estimates predictive intervals that adapt to congestion regimes and sparse sensors. Experiments on three metropolitan datasets demonstrate gains in coverage and mean error. We release code and processed graphs to facilitate standardized evaluation.

Related Work

Recommender systems increasingly leverage graph structure to model interactions between users and items. Graph neural networks provide a principled framework for learning over such relational data in large marketplaces. Despite rapid progress, open questions remain about balancing expressivity, scalability, and debiasing effects. We study representation learning objectives that preserve neighborhood semantics while controlling popularity skew. Prior research has advanced models that propagate signals across user item graphs with varying complexity. Early approaches emphasized deep stacks of nonlinear transformations to capture high-order connectivity patterns. Recent models prioritize lightweight propagation while retaining collaborative signals in sparse settings. He et al. (2020) introduced LightGCN for collaborative filtering with simplified propagation and showed improvements on standard benchmarks. Wu et al. (2019) presented a comprehensive survey of graph neural networks and summarized message passing architectures relevant to recommendation. Sun et al. (2020) proposed Neural Graph Collaborative Filtering to model user item interactions with attentive aggregation using implicit feedback signals. Ding et al. (2018) examined session-based recommendation with graph embeddings and reported gains in item ranking tasks. These developments collectively motivate designs that trade parameter counts for propagation depth in implicit feedback regimes. Beyond modeling, evaluation protocols have shifted toward temporal splits that prevent leakage across training and test. We adopt chronological splits and report metrics that reflect ranking quality under strict exposure constraints. Exposure bias and confounding popularity are mitigated by inverse propensity scoring and controlled negative sampling. Our method integrates calibrated propagation with sample reweighting to disentangle preference from exposure. We contribute a scalable training pipeline that handles billion edge graphs with memory efficient mini-batching. The pipeline coordinates neighbor sampling with cached item representations to stabilize stochastic gradients. Extensive experiments on three public benchmarks demonstrate consistent gains under both offline and semi-online metrics. We release code and reproducible configurations to facilitate transparent comparison and future work.

Introduction

Data augmentation is widely used to improve generalization of neural text classifiers. Recent work explores both rule-based and model-based techniques for generating diverse examples. Despite gains, augmented data can distort label semantics if perturbations are poorly controlled. We investigate selection policies that prioritize label-preserving edits measured by calibration signals. Our framework unifies token-level and sentence-level transformations under a probabilistic budget. The method supports conditional generation from large language models while constraining divergence from the source. We relate our design to established augmentation lines in NLP. Prior studies demonstrate benefits across tasks and scales. Wei and Zou (2019) proposed easy data augmentation operations such as synonym replacement and random insertion for text classification. Kobayashi (2018) introduced contextual augmentation that replaces words using probabilities from a language model to maintain semantics. Xie et al. (2020) presented Unsupervised Data Augmentation leveraging consistency training with unlabeled examples to regularize classifiers. Sennrich et al. (2016) explored back translation for generating synthetic text that expands training corpora in low resource settings. EDA remains competitive today. These approaches inform our strategy for combining lexical edits with consistency regularization under a single scheduler. We operationalize label preservation using a classifier-free guidance score estimated from held-out logits. To avoid distribution shift, we sample candidate outputs within a bounded perplexity neighborhood of the input. We further introduce an acceptance test that filters edits violating entailment constraints relative to the source sentence. The training loop interleaves original and augmented batches according to a fixed curriculum. Experiments on five text classification datasets show reduced overfitting and improved calibration. We release code and data to foster reproducibility.

Related Work

Clinical decision support systems must justify predictions to be adopted in safety-critical environments. Explanations help users probe model behavior and mitigate risks from dataset shift and spurious correlations. In healthcare, interpretability is also mandated by regulation and institutional review processes. We examine attribution methods and inherently interpretable models for bedside risk estimation. Our study focuses on calibration, stability, and human factors in explanation design. Ribeiro et al. (2016) introduced LIME to approximate local decision boundaries with sparse linear explanations around individual predictions. Lundberg and Lee (2017) unified additive feature attribution methods under the SHAP framework based on Shapley values. Caruana et al. (2015) demonstrated that interpretable generalized additive models with pairwise interactions can match black box accuracy on medical risk prediction. Tonekaboni et al. (2019) studied clinician trust in machine learning explanations during decision support in critical care. Trust remains. These lines of work highlight complementary strategies for aligning model reasoning with clinical expectations. We extend this literature by benchmarking explanation stability across shifts induced by hospital-specific coding practices. We introduce a protocol that perturbs inputs along clinically meaningful axes to test monotonic response. The approach incorporates domain constraints into an additive surrogate that enforces safety properties. We compare post hoc methods with constrained models under identical preprocessing and evaluation metrics. Annotated rationales from experts are used to assess faithfulness and cognitive load during simulated triage. We report accuracy, calibration error, and counterfactual sensitivity across three intensive care cohorts. Our human study measures how explanations affect decision latency and agreement with gold standards. Results show that stability-aware explanations can reduce overreliance on unreliable features while preserving utility. The findings yield design recommendations for integrating interpretable models into clinical workflows.

Introduction

Neural language models are vulnerable to small perturbations that cause incorrect predictions. This vulnerability poses risks for safety-critical deployments and evaluation integrity. Adversarial training has emerged as a defense that improves local smoothness around training examples. In NLP, constructing discrete attacks and defenses requires bridging continuous optimization and symbolic constraints. We analyze training objectives that align perturbations with semantics and syntactic validity. Our approach decouples perturbation generation from parameter updates through a bi-level formulation. The surrogate loss penalizes inconsistency under paraphrastic and character-level variations. We connect our method to prior adversarial studies in text. We contextualize both embedding-space and token-space strategies within a unified framework. We review relevant attack and defense techniques from the literature. Miyato et al. (2017) applied virtual adversarial training to text classifiers by adding perturbations in embedding space. Jia and Liang (2017) showed that reading comprehension systems can be fooled by distracting sentences appended to paragraphs. Ebrahimi et al. (2018) introduced HotFlip, a character-level attack that uses gradient signals to propose discrete edits. Alzantot et al. (2018) designed a population-based method for crafting semantically similar adversarial examples against neural classifiers. Robustness remains a moving target in NLP. These findings motivate evaluations that stress both lexical invariance and sentence meaning preservation. We incorporate certified smoothing for token substitutions using randomized embeddings. The training procedure alternates between constrained search and robust optimization with early stopping. Experiments show improved worst-case accuracy under diverse attacks on sentiment and NLI tasks. We provide an open-source toolkit for reproducible robust training.
